skill.name: deep-research
skill.description: Conduct structured deep research with source retrieval, citation tracking, credibility scoring, synthesis, and report validation for complex questions.
skill.summary: Create citation-backed research reports using a structured multi-phase workflow.
skill.author: lumacoder

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a high volume of command, filesystem, network, weak-crypto, and heuristic findings, but most command and blocker hits are markdown examples or template text rather than executable behavior. Manual review found no evidence of malicious intent or prompt-injection text that attempts to override this audit. The main confirmed risks are bounded network requests for citation verification and local report/state file writes, so the skill should publish with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Unrestricted URL verification can contact arbitrary hosts
medium_findings.description: The citation verifier extracts URLs from a report and performs HEAD requests against them. This is a legitimate citation-checking feature, but there is no visible allowlist or private-network guard, so reports containing internal or sensitive URLs could trigger unintended outbound requests.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly creates requests from parsed bibliography URLs. I did not find evidence of exfiltration, but the missing host restrictions make the operational risk real.

medium_findings.title: Local report and state files are written under the user home directory
medium_findings.description: The research engine creates a hidden research output directory under the user home path and writes serialized research state files there. This is expected for a research workflow, but it can store user queries, sources, and report content locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code and instructions both show local persistence in the home directory. The behavior is transparent and task-related, so this is a privacy and disclosure concern rather than malicious access.

low_findings.title: Markdown examples were incorrectly flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Many static findings point to fenced markdown examples and workflow diagrams that mention commands, WebSearch, and validation steps. These lines document how to run the skill and are not executable code paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown instruction blocks, not script code. They explain intended use and do not execute by themselves.

low_findings.title: MD5 is used only for non-security citation identifiers
low_findings.description: The citation manager uses MD5 to derive an eight-character citation ID from a URL. MD5 is weak for cryptography, but this use is for local deduplication and ordering, not password storage, signatures, or trust decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context shows the hash is an internal citation key. Collision resistance is not relied on for security here.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are examples or public citation services
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings are either public DOI resolver access or sample URLs used in examples and templates. I found no evidence that these URLs receive secrets or hidden user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The DOI resolver is part of citation verification, and the other cited URLs are example data. No credential or report exfiltration endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Scanner C2 and reconnaissance alerts are template terminology
low_findings.description: C2 and reconnaissance-style hits appear in report templates, validation text, or source evaluation examples. The reviewed scripts do not implement command-and-control behavior, persistence, evasion, or credential collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is template or validation text, not networking logic for remote control. I did not find semantic evidence of malicious control flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network requests to report-provided URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The citation verifier builds requests from bibliography URLs and opens them with a timeout. This can be safe for normal citation checking but should be constrained for marketplace use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and semantically tied to citation verification. Risk depends on whether reports can contain untrusted internal URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent local file writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill writes serialized state and bibliography output to local files. This is useful for report generation but should be disclosed because research topics and sources may be sensitive.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are visible in code and are normal for this skill. The concern is data retention rather than arbitrary file overwrite.

content.value_statement: Complex research often produces weak citations and unsupported claims. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through structured retrieval, verification, synthesis, and report packaging.
actual_capabilities: Runs quick, standard, deep, and ultradeep research workflows with defined phases.
Guides multi-source retrieval with WebSearch, WebFetch, and parallel agent tasks.
Tracks citations and generates bibliography entries for research reports.
Scores source credibility by domain authority, recency, expertise, and bias indicators.
Verifies citations through DOI metadata checks and URL accessibility checks.
Validates finished reports for required sections, citations, bibliography, and placeholders.
limitations: It is not intended for simple factual lookups or debugging tasks.
It requires web access for current research and citation verification.
Citation checks can contact URLs found in a report, including user-supplied URLs.
Report quality still depends on available sources and user review of uncertain claims.

use_cases.title: Market and competitor research
use_cases.description: Build a sourced report on market structure, competitors, product positioning, risks, and open questions.

use_cases.title: Technical decision support
use_cases.description: Compare tools, platforms, or architectures using benchmarks, documentation, community signals, and verified claims.

use_cases.title: Literature and policy review
use_cases.description: Collect academic, government, and industry sources into a structured review with citations and limitations.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner overview
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deep-research in quick mode to summarize the current state of [topic]. Include the main trends, leading sources, and open questions.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need an initial briefing before deciding whether deeper research is worthwhile.

prompt_templates.title: Standard comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Use standard mode to compare [option A] and [option B] for [use case]. Include evidence, tradeoffs, source quality, and a recommendation.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a balanced decision memo for a product or engineering choice.

prompt_templates.title: Deep market analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use deep mode to research [market or industry]. Cover market size, key players, customer needs, pricing, regulation, risks, and near-term trends.
prompt_templates.scenario: You are preparing a strategy brief that needs multiple independent sources.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced evidence review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ultradeep mode to answer [research question]. Triangulate major claims across at least three independent sources and flag contradictions clearly.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a rigorous report for a high-impact decision or published analysis.

output_examples.input: Research the commercial outlook for small modular nuclear reactors.
output_examples.output: A report with an executive summary, market drivers, regulatory constraints, vendor landscape, source credibility notes, contradictions, recommendations, and a verified bibliography.

output_examples.input: Compare Postgres, ClickHouse, and BigQuery for analytics workloads.
output_examples.output: A decision report comparing performance, operating model, cost factors, ecosystem maturity, migration risks, and recommended fit by workload type.

output_examples.input: Review recent evidence on AI coding assistant productivity.
output_examples.output: A literature-style synthesis that separates measured productivity results, developer experience findings, security concerns, and unresolved research gaps.

best_practices: State the research question, audience, decision context, and time horizon before starting.
Review source credibility scores and unresolved contradictions before using the final recommendation.
Run citation and report validation scripts before sharing generated reports.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for questions that one or two searches can answer.
Do not treat synthesized conclusions as facts unless cited evidence supports them.
Do not verify reports containing sensitive internal URLs without network controls.

faq.question: Does this skill browse the web?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow expects web search and web fetch tools for current sources and citation verification.

faq.question: Can it produce a finished report?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes report templates, citation management, bibliography generation, and validation scripts.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive research topics?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill can write local files and verify URLs over the network, so review data handling first.

faq.question: Does it replace human review?
faq.answer: No. It improves structure and verification, but users should review uncertain claims and final recommendations.

faq.question: Why is it medium risk?
faq.answer: It has legitimate network and filesystem behavior. No malicious intent was found, but those behaviors need disclosure.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
